name: A Step-by-Step Guide to Filing a Workplace Hearing Loss Claim

step.name: Step 1
step.text:
Hearing loss is one of the most common yet underreported workplace injuries in Utah and Idaho. Whether you work in a loud manufacturing plant in West Jordan, a construction site in St. George, or a warehouse in Boise, constant exposure to high-decibel noise can permanently damage your hearing. This "How-To" guide explains the steps you need to take to secure the benefits you deserve.

Step 1: Get a Professional Audiogram

If you notice you are turning the TV up louder or struggling to understand conversations in noisy rooms, schedule an appointment with an Audiologist or an ENT (Ear, Nose, and Throat) specialist. A standard hearing screening isn't enough; you need a full diagnostic audiogram that tests multiple frequencies. Key Tip: Ask the doctor to specifically note if your hearing loss pattern is consistent with "noise-induced" damage.

Step 2: Compare to Your Baseline (If Available)

Many employers conduct "baseline" hearing tests when you are hired. If you can, get a copy of that original test. By comparing your current hearing loss to your baseline, you can show a clear decline that occurred during your tenure at the company. If you don't have a baseline, your doctor can still provide an opinion based on the specific "notch" in your hearing frequencies often seen in industrial workers.

Step 3: Formal Notification to Your Employer

Once you have a diagnosis, you must notify your supervisor or HR department in writing. State clearly that you believe your hearing loss was caused by the noise levels at work. In Utah and Idaho, failing to provide this notice within a specific timeframe (usually 30 to 60 days from the diagnosis) can jeopardize your claim.

Step 4: File the Workers\' Compensation Claim

After you report the injury, the employer\'s insurance company will open a file. They will likely ask you questions about your hobbies. Be honest, but be aware that they are looking for reasons to blame your hearing loss on things like hunting, concerts, or aging. Their goal is to prove your loss is "non-industrial."

Step 5: Demand Future Medical Care

The biggest mistake workers make is settling for a one-time cash payment. Hearing aids are expensive ($3,000–$7,000 per pair) and they need to be replaced every 5 to 7 years. A proper workers\' compensation settlement must include a provision for "future medical care" that covers hearing aid replacements and audiologist visits for the rest of your life.

Step 6: Consult with an Attorney

If the insurance company denies your claim, says your hearing loss is just "getting old," or refuses to pay for the high-quality hearing aids you need, you should contact a lawyer. We can file an Application for Hearing with the state labor board to fight for your rights.

description: A Step-by-Step Guide to Filing a Workplace Hearing Loss Claim - Taylorsville
